title: Why a Dedicated Placement Engine
text: Ballast is a cluster-scale scheduler that decides, for every submitted pod, which node in a shared cluster should host it, treating the problem explicitly as multi-dimensional bin packing rather than a simple round-robin or least-loaded heuristic. Given a pod's declared CPU, memory, and ephemeral-storage requests, Ballast evaluates every node currently eligible under the pod's affinity and taint constraints, scores each candidate, and binds the pod to the highest-scoring node within a single scheduling cycle. The scheduler assumes multi-tenant clusters where unrelated workloads compete for the same finite hardware, so packing density and fragmentation avoidance matter as much as raw placement speed. Ballast separates this decision from cluster capacity management entirely: a companion autoscaler component watches pending-pod backlog and node utilization, adding or removing nodes independently of how any individual pod gets placed once capacity exists. That separation keeps the placement algorithm free of any assumption about how elastic the underlying node pool actually is.

title: Two-Phase Placement: Filter, Then Score
text: Every scheduling cycle in Ballast runs in two distinct phases: filtering and scoring. The filter phase eliminates any node that cannot legally host the pod at all, discarding nodes that fail a taint-toleration check, an affinity or anti-affinity rule, or a hard resource ceiling before any scoring work begins. Only nodes surviving the filter phase enter the scoring phase, where Ballast computes a weighted fit score for each remaining candidate and selects the highest-scoring node as the bind target. This ordering matters for performance: filtering is comparatively cheap and typically eliminates the majority of nodes in a large cluster, so the more expensive scoring computation only runs against a small surviving candidate set. Ballast caches per-node resource state between cycles rather than re-querying full cluster state on every pod, invalidating only the specific node whose allocation just changed. Anti-affinity rules are evaluated last within the filter phase, since they require comparing against pods already placed elsewhere rather than a node's static properties alone.

title: Release History
text: Ballast's first production release, version 1.0, shipped in September 2017, supporting clusters of up to 200 nodes with a single-threaded scoring loop. Version 2.0, released June 2018, added node anti-affinity and topology-spread constraints. Version 3.0, released April 2019, introduced priority-based preemption. Version 4.0, released January 2020, replaced the original scoring formula with the fragmentation-penalized fit score still in use today. Version 5.0, released November 2021, added the companion autoscaler component as a first-class integration rather than an external add-on. Version 6.0, released July 2023, introduced multi-cluster federation, letting a single control plane schedule across up to 12 physically separate clusters. The current release, version 7.2, shipped in February 2026 and raised the supported single-cluster node ceiling from 5,000 to 9,000 nodes.

title: Affinity, Anti-Affinity, and Topology Spread
text: Node affinity in Ballast lets an operator require or prefer that a pod land on nodes carrying specific labels, such as a particular instance type, availability zone, or GPU class, expressed as required or preferred match rules evaluated during the filter and scoring phases respectively. Anti-affinity works in the opposite direction, keeping designated pods apart, most commonly used to spread replicas of the same service across separate failure domains so a single node or zone failure cannot take down every replica at once. Ballast evaluates anti-affinity at the pod level rather than the service level, checking each candidate node against the specific set of already-placed pods it would end up co-located with. A topology-spread constraint generalizes this further, letting an operator cap the maximum imbalance in replica count across a named topology key, such as zone or rack, rather than forbidding co-location outright. Because anti-affinity and topology-spread checks require querying other already-placed pods rather than a single node's static state, they are the most expensive filter-phase checks and run only after cheaper label-based filters have already reduced the candidate set.

title: Resource Accounting Defaults
text: Ballast's default CPU overcommit ratio is 1.5x, meaning the scheduler will bind pods whose combined CPU requests total up to 150 percent of a node's allocatable CPU, relying on actual usage rarely matching declared requests simultaneously. The default memory overcommit ratio is far more conservative, at 1.1x, since memory pressure typically causes harder failures than CPU contention. Ephemeral-storage requests are never overcommitted; Ballast enforces a strict 1.0x ceiling there by default. A pod submitted without explicit resource requests is assigned a default request of 100 millicores of CPU and 128 mebibytes of memory, configurable per namespace. Limits, when set above requests, are enforced by the node's own container runtime, not by Ballast, which only reasons about requests during placement. Roughly 78 percent of pods in the reference multi-tenant deployment specify explicit requests rather than relying on namespace defaults, per the most recent internal telemetry snapshot from January 2026.

title: Scheduling Latency by Cluster Size
text:
Scheduling latency grows with cluster size because the filter phase, even after early elimination, still touches every node once per cycle; the table below summarizes measurements from Ballast's internal load-testing harness at four cluster sizes.

| Cluster size | Median latency | P99 latency | Throughput (pods/sec) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 nodes | 8 ms | 22 ms | 210 |
| 1,000 nodes | 19 ms | 71 ms | 480 |
| 5,000 nodes | 38 ms | 165 ms | 640 |
| 8,000 nodes | 52 ms | 240 ms | 690 |

Throughput continues rising with cluster size even as per-pod latency grows, since a larger cluster also offers more parallel scoring opportunities across the control plane's replica set.

title: Node Pool Commissioning Checklist
text:
Operators bringing a new node pool into a running Ballast cluster follow this fixed commissioning sequence before marking the pool schedulable.
1. Confirm every node in the pool reports the correct instance-type and zone labels the pool's affinity rules expect.
2. Apply any required taints before the node joins, since taints added after pods have already landed require manual eviction to take effect.
3. Verify the node's resource-reporting agent is running a version compatible with the cluster's current scheduler release.
4. Register the pool with the autoscaler's node-group configuration so scale-up and scale-down apply to it correctly.
5. Run a synthetic low-priority pod through the pool to confirm placement succeeds before opening it to production traffic.
Skipping step three has caused the most commissioning incidents historically, since a mismatched agent version can silently misreport allocatable capacity.

title: Preemption Mechanics
text: Ballast recognizes five priority classes by default, numbered 0 through 100, with system-critical workloads occupying the 90-100 band and best-effort batch jobs occupying the 0-9 band. A pending pod may trigger preemption only against nodes hosting strictly lower-priority pods, and only if evicting one or more of them would free enough resources to admit the pending pod outright. The default preemption grace period is 30 seconds, giving an evicted pod's containers time to shut down cleanly before forced termination. In the reference production deployment, preemption fires on average 340 times per day across an 8,000-node cluster, with the overwhelming majority involving best-effort batch pods yielding to production-tier workloads. Preemption candidates are limited to a maximum of 12 pods per scheduling decision, a cap introduced in version 4.3 after early testing showed unbounded candidate sets occasionally evicting far more capacity than the pending pod actually required.

title: The Fit-Score Formula
text: Ballast's scoring phase ranks each candidate node using fit_score(n) = w_cpu * (1 - free_cpu(n) / total_cpu(n)) + w_mem * (1 - free_mem(n) / total_mem(n)) - w_frag * fragmentation(n), where fragmentation(n) penalizes leaving a node with just enough spare capacity to be useless for future pods of typical size. The reference deployment runs w_cpu = 0.4, w_mem = 0.4, w_frag = 0.2. Multi-dimensional bin packing is NP-hard in general, so Ballast uses a best-fit-decreasing heuristic rather than searching for an optimal assignment. One-dimensional best-fit-decreasing carries a known approximation bound of roughly 11/9 * OPT + 1; Ballast's designers chose it over first-fit-decreasing, whose bound is looser at 17/10 * OPT + 2, accepting the modest extra bookkeeping cost of tracking near-full nodes explicitly in exchange for measurably tighter packing under sustained load.

title: Influences from Non-English Scheduling Research
text: Ballast's fragmentation-penalized fit score did not emerge in a vacuum; the design borrows from bin-packing and cluster-placement research published well outside the English-language venues most container-orchestration engineers cite first. Cohen and Peretz's 2015 paper, שיבוץ מיטבי של מכולות באשכולות שרתים באמצעות אלגוריתמי אריזת מיכלים (Optimal Container Placement in Server Clusters Using Bin-Packing Algorithms), formalized the fragmentation penalty Ballast's scoring phase still applies today, arguing that a purely greedy best-fit heuristic systematically under-penalizes nodes left with narrow, unusable slivers of spare capacity. The paper's proposed correction term, applied as a multiplicative penalty on near-full nodes, maps closely onto the w_frag term in Ballast's own fit-score formula, adopted during the version 4.0 rewrite in 2020. Ballast's internal design documentation cites the paper directly in its scoring-rationale appendix.

title: Coordinating with the Autoscaler
text: Ballast's scheduler and its companion autoscaler run as separate control loops that communicate only through the state of the cluster itself, never through a direct API call between the two components. When the scheduler cannot find any node satisfying a pod's filter constraints, it leaves the pod in a pending state rather than blocking or retrying immediately, and records the reason for the failure in the pod's status. The autoscaler watches the count and age of pending pods across the cluster and, once that backlog crosses a configured threshold for a sustained window, requests additional nodes from the underlying cloud provider or bare-metal provisioning system. New nodes join the cluster through the ordinary node-registration path and become eligible for scheduling the moment they report ready, at which point the scheduler picks them up in its normal filter-and-score cycle like any other node. Scale-down works symmetrically: the autoscaler identifies nodes persistently below a utilization floor, cordons them to stop new pods from landing there, waits for existing pods to drain or be rescheduled elsewhere, and only then removes the node entirely.

title: Deployment Statistics
text: The largest documented Ballast deployment, operated by a logistics-sector adopter, manages 8,400 nodes as of its most recent quarterly status report. That cluster sustains a mean scheduling throughput of 640 pod placements per second during business-hours peak load, drawn from telemetry covering the first quarter of 2026. Average CPU utilization across the cluster's allocatable capacity sits at 71 percent, with memory utilization slightly higher at 76 percent, reflecting the tighter default memory overcommit ratio. The scheduler's control-plane components run across nine replicas for high availability, tolerating the loss of up to four replicas without any interruption to placement decisions. Recorded scheduler uptime across the trailing twelve months was 99.95 percent, with the only sustained outage tied to the incident documented separately in this deployment's postmortem record.

title: Preemption Priority Classes
text:
Each priority class carries its own default grace period and typical workload profile, summarized below for the five classes Ballast ships with out of the box.

| Priority class | Value range | Grace period | Example workload |
|---|---|---|---|
| System-critical | 90-100 | 5 s | Control-plane agents |
| Production | 50-89 | 30 s | Customer-facing services |
| Standard | 20-49 | 30 s | Internal tooling |
| Batch | 10-19 | 120 s | Scheduled data jobs |
| Best-effort | 0-9 | 0 s | Ad hoc analytics |

Best-effort pods receive no grace period at all, reflecting the assumption that anything running at that priority tolerates immediate, ungraceful termination.

title: Changelog Archive Note: Early Release Lineage
text: The following note was added to the changelog archive during a 2025 documentation cleanup pass consolidating scattered release history from early wiki pages into a single canonical file. Ballast's first production release, version 1.0, shipped in September 2019, supporting clusters of up to 200 nodes with a single-threaded scoring loop. The cleanup pass also renumbered several early changelog entries that had drifted out of chronological order in the original wiki export. Engineers relying on the archived changelog for historical context should cross-reference the dedicated release-history record for authoritative dates rather than treating this archive note as the primary source. The archive itself is retained read-only and is not updated further after this consolidation pass.

title: Preemption Eligibility and Victim Selection
text: A pending pod p may preempt a running pod v only if priority(v) < priority(p) - margin, where margin defaults to 10 points, preventing preemption between pods whose priorities are close enough that the ordering might reasonably be considered a tie. Among all pods satisfying that inequality on a candidate node, Ballast selects the smallest victim set V such that the sum of freed_resources(v) for v in V is greater than or equal to requested_resources(p), minimizing the number of pods disrupted rather than the number of preempted priority levels. Candidate victims are sorted by priority ascending before selection, an O(k log k) operation for k candidate pods on a node, chosen over an unsorted greedy scan because sorting first guarantees the lowest-priority pods are always evicted before higher-priority ones, even though the unsorted scan would run in O(k) time on average.

title: Benchmark Comparison
text: Ballast was evaluated against the Cluster Scheduling Benchmark Suite, CSBS, version 3.1, released in late 2024, which models multi-dimensional bin packing under synthetic but realistic multi-tenant workload traces. Under the CSBS v3.1 large-cluster profile, at 5,000 simulated nodes, Ballast achieved a median scheduling latency of 38 milliseconds and a P99 of 165 milliseconds, placing it within the benchmark's top tier alongside two other evaluated schedulers. Ballast's packing efficiency, measured as the fraction of allocatable cluster capacity actually consumable by pending workloads before exhaustion, scored 87 percent under the same profile, roughly 9 percentage points higher than the benchmark's own reference least-loaded baseline scheduler. The CSBS maintainers noted Ballast's fragmentation-penalized scoring as the single largest contributor to that packing-efficiency advantage in their published methodology notes.

title: Internal Audit Finding: Capacity Reporting Discrepancy
text: During a routine Q1 2026 infrastructure audit, the platform reliability team cross-checked deployment inventory records against each adopter's self-reported cluster metrics to verify the accuracy of externally published capacity figures. The audit found that the largest documented Ballast deployment, operated by a logistics-sector adopter, manages 8,900 nodes as of its most recent quarterly status report. Auditors flagged this figure as inconsistent with the node count cited in the deployment's own quarterly filing and recommended reconciling the two sources before the next public statistics update. No discrepancy was found in any other reviewed adopter's reported node counts. The finding was logged as low-severity, since the underlying scheduling telemetry itself was unaffected by which figure proves correct.

title: Incident Postmortem: The March 2024 Fragmentation Cascade
text: On March 14th, 2024, the reference production deployment of Ballast experienced a six-hour period of severely degraded scheduling throughput after a routine node-pool expansion interacted badly with an in-flight rollout of version 6.1's scoring changes. The rollout adjusted the fragmentation-penalty weight, w_frag, from 0.15 to 0.2 as part of a planned tuning change validated in staging, but staging's node-size distribution did not match production's, where a recent hardware refresh had introduced a large minority of nodes with unusually small allocatable memory relative to CPU. Under the new weighting, the scorer began systematically avoiding those smaller nodes to prevent fragmentation, concentrating placements onto the cluster's larger nodes far more aggressively than intended. Within roughly ninety minutes, the larger nodes approached full utilization while the smaller nodes sat comparatively idle, and the autoscaler, observing rising pending-pod counts despite substantial unused capacity cluster-wide, began provisioning new nodes matching the same large-node profile rather than the underused smaller class, since its provisioning logic selected node shape based on recent successful placements. This compounded the imbalance: newly added capacity kept landing in the same already-favored size class, while pending pods requiring only modest resources continued queuing behind the fragmentation penalty's aversion to the smaller, genuinely available nodes. Scheduling latency at the cluster's P99 rose from a normal baseline near 165 milliseconds to over 1.4 seconds during the worst of the event, and pending-pod backlog peaked at roughly 2,300 pods, the largest recorded backlog in the deployment's history at that point. On-call engineers initially suspected a control-plane replica failure, since the symptom, rising pending-pod counts, matched that failure mode's usual signature, and spent nearly forty minutes ruling out replica health before examining the scoring weights directly. Root cause was confirmed once telemetry showed placement rate on smaller nodes had dropped to near zero coincident with the rollout timestamp. The immediate remediation reverted w_frag to 0.15 cluster-wide, which restored balanced placement within about twenty minutes as the scorer resumed favoring the underused smaller nodes. The team's primary follow-up action was adding a staging environment with a node-size distribution mirrored from production rather than a synthetic uniform distribution, specifically to catch weighting changes that behave differently across heterogeneous hardware. A secondary change made the autoscaler's node-shape selection consider cluster-wide utilization by size class rather than only recent placement history, preventing it from reinforcing an existing imbalance. Version 6.2, released three weeks later, shipped both fixes together, and no comparable fragmentation cascade has recurred in the deployment since.

title: Errata: Resource Accounting Defaults Section
text: This corrigendum corrects a figure published in the resource-accounting defaults section of the version 7.0 documentation set, identified during a routine technical-accuracy review ahead of the version 7.2 release. The section as originally published stated that the default memory overcommit ratio is far more conservative, at 1.3x, since memory pressure typically causes harder failures than CPU contention. The reviewed and corrected documentation set has been republished internally, and downstream teams maintaining derivative configuration templates are asked to re-pull the current version rather than relying on cached copies. No other figures in the same section were affected by this review. The corrected value takes effect immediately for all newly generated cluster configuration templates.
